coredata.dc:title: RuZn NPs with electroactivity and oxidase-like property for dual-mode anti-cancer drug monitoring
coredata.dc:creator: Jin, Zhao
Peng, Fang
Du, Qiaodan
Liang, Dan
Zhao, Yuan
coredata.dc:description: 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP) as the effective anti-cancer drug was used for the treatment of Crohn's disease and acute lymphoblastic leukaemia, but the response to maintenance therapy was variable with individual differences. In order to control the dosage and decrease the side effects of 6-MP, a sensitive and stable assay was urgently needed for 6-MP monitoring. Herein, RuZn NPs with electrochemical oxidation property and oxidase-like activity was proposed for dual-mode 6-MP monitoring. Burr-like RuZn NPs were prepared and explored to not only exhibit an electrochemical oxidation signal at 0.78 V, but also displayed excellent oxidase-like performances. RuZn NPs were utilized for the dual-mode monitoring of 6-MP, attributing to the formation of Ru-SH covalent bonding. The colorimetric method showed good linearity from 10 μM to 5 mM with the limit of detection (LOD) of 300 nM, while the electrochemical method provided a higher sensitivity with the LOD of 37 nM in range from 100 nM to 200 μM. This work provided a new way for the fabrication of dual-functional nanotags with electroactivity and oxidase-like property, and opened a dual-mode approach for the 6-MP detection applications with complementary and satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: RuZn NPs
Electroactivity
Oxidase-like property
Dual-mode sensor
Anti-cancer drugs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The oxidase-like performances of RuZn NPs
Stability assessment of the electrochemical activity and oxidase properties of RuZn NPs
RuZn NPs engineered electrochemical sensor for 6-MP monitoring
RuZn NPs engineered colorimetric sensor for 6-MP monitoring
Synthesis and characterization of RuZn NPs
Electrochemical oxidation properties of RuZn NPs
The oxidase-like properties of RuZn NPs
Study of the stability of RuZn NPs
Construction of dual-mode sensor for 6-MP monitoring

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:Yuan Zhao reports financial support was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.The authors acknowledged the financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22278184).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–c) TEM images of Ru NPs (a), Zn NPs (b) and RuZn NPs (c). Inset in a-c, the size statistics and physical images of NPs. (d–g) HAADF-STEM images of RuZn NPs. (h) EDX spectra of RuZn NPs with insets showing the elemental representation tables.

(a–c) XPS patterns of RuZn NPs. (d) The electrochemical oxidization curves of Ru NPs, ZnNPs and RuZn NPs. (e) The electrochemical reduction curves of Ru NPs, ZnNPs and RuZn NPs. Inset in d-e, the schematic illustration of the electron transfer process of RuZn NPs. (f) The electrochemical oxidization current at 0.78 V of RuZn NPs modified at eight different GCE.

(a) The UV–vis absorption spectra of 0.1 M PBS solution after the addition of 1) RuZn NPs, 2) TMB, 3) RuZn NPs and TMB. Inset, the corresponding color changes. (b) The UV–vis absorption spectra of TMB solution in the presence of RuZn NPs without (1) and with (2) N2. Inset, the corresponding color changes. (c) The UV–vis absorption spectra of TMB solution in the presence of RuZn NPs and different trapping agents. Inset, 1, 2, 3, 4 represented the corresponding photographs of the reaction systems without (1) and with different trapping agents of TBA (2), His (3) and PBQ (4), respectively. (d) ESR spectra of TEMP and TEMP + RuZn NPs. (e) Schematic representation of the catalytic oxidization of TMB in the presence of RuZn NPs. (f) The UV–vis absorption spectra of TMB solution after the addition of Ru NPs, Zn NPs and RuZn NPs. Inset, the corresponding photograph of the reaction system.
(a) DPV curves of RuZn NPs in the presence of different interference ions. (b) The UV–vis absorption spectra of TMB solution in the presence of RuZn NPs and different interference ions.
Schematic representation of RuZn NPs engineered electrochemical/colourimetric dual-mode sensor for the detection of 6-MP.

(a) The DPV curves of RuZn NPs with and without of 100 μM 6-MP. (b) The UV–vis absorption spectra of TMB catalytic system in the presence of RuZn NPs with and without of 100 μM 6-MP. Inset, the photographs of TMB solution with and without of 100 μM 6-MP. (c) The DPV response of the electrochemical sensor at different 6-MP concentrations. (d) The UV–vis absorption spectra of TMB catalytic system in the presence of RuZn NPs at different 6-MP concentrations. (e) The linear relationship between the logarithm of the concentration of 6-MP and the current at 0.78 V of RuZn NPs. (f) The linear relationship between the logarithm of the concentration of 6-MP and the summary of absorption peaks at 370 nm, 453 nm and 650 nm. Inset, the corresponding photograph of TMB catalytic system in the presence of different concentrations of 6-MP.

head.ce:title: RuZn NPs with electroactivity and oxidase-like property for dual-mode anti-cancer drug monitoring

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, Jiangsu, 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids
School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, Jiangsu, 214122, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP) as the effective anti-cancer drug was used for the treatment of Crohn's disease and acute lymphoblastic leukaemia, but the response to maintenance therapy was variable with individual differences. In order to control the dosage and decrease the side effects of 6-MP, a sensitive and stable assay was urgently needed for 6-MP monitoring. Herein, RuZn NPs with electrochemical oxidation property and oxidase-like activity was proposed for dual-mode 6-MP monitoring. Burr-like RuZn NPs were prepared and explored to not only exhibit an electrochemical oxidation signal at 0.78 V, but also displayed excellent oxidase-like performances. RuZn NPs were utilized for the dual-mode monitoring of 6-MP, attributing to the formation of Ru-SH covalent bonding. The colorimetric method showed good linearity from 10 μM to 5 mM with the limit of detection (LOD) of 300 nM, while the electrochemical method provided a higher sensitivity with the LOD of 37 nM in range from 100 nM to 200 μM. This work provided a new way for the fabrication of dual-functional nanotags with electroactivity and oxidase-like property, and opened a dual-mode approach for the 6-MP detection applications with complementary and satisfactory results.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP, C5H4N4S) is a drug that can effectively treat Crohn's disease, inflammatory bowel disease and acute lymphoblastic leukaemia [1]. However, its plasma concentration is low and the response to maintenance therapy varies from person to person [2,3]. There are some non-negligible side effects, such as damage to liver, bone marrow suppression, leukocyte and platelet reductions [4,5]. Therefore, in order to decrease the side effects, a sensitive and stable assay is urgently needed for the accurate monitoring of 6-MP in human serum and controlling the dosage of 6-MP.

To date, a number of methods have been developed for the detection of 6-MP, such as high performance liquid chromatography, liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, capillary electrophoresis, Raman spectroscopy, ultraviolet–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy [5–10]. These methods have their own drawbacks, such as cumbersome operation, poor sensitivity and the need for expensive instruments. What's more, all these detection methods mainly depend on the single detection mode, whose results are easily interfered by the external environment and face a considerable challenge to the accuracy [11,12]. Alternatively, dual-mode detection employs different detection signals, and shows better accuracy and reliability for the detection applications [13–15]. Different detection signals could provide a complemented approach for the stable and reliable 6-MP monitoring. Therefore, it has been a challenge for analytical researchers to develop a simple, low-cost and reliable method for dual-mode 6-MP detection.

In particular, electrochemical analysis has the advantages of simple operation and high sensitivity, and is widely used in environmental monitoring and biological and food analysis [16–20]. Colorimetry has simple detection steps, wide applicability of the instrument and low cost of analysis [21–23]. Therefore, it is of interest to combine the electrochemical and UV signals to invent a dual-mode response sensor for detecting 6-MP with accurate and complementary results. Different detection modes can be used in different demanding situations. Thus, the design of detection nanotags with electrochemical response and catalytic properties is important for the development of dual-mode detection sensors.

Among the noble metal elements, ruthenium (Ru) element not only has excellent electro-oxidizing properties [24–26], but also shows excellent catalytic performance in the fields of catalytic hydrogenation, catalytic ammonia synthesis, and catalytic water decomposition. Individual Ru nanocatalysts also are less stable and prone to aggregation, which reduce their catalytic activity [27]. The construction of Ru-containing multimetallic nanocatalysts by introducing other non-precious metal elements not only can significantly reduce the content of Ru element, but the formation of the polymetallic alloy structure will produce a synergistic effect between different metals and significantly improve its catalytic activity and stability. In particular, Ru-based nanocatalysts, such as Ru–ZrO2 on nitrogen-doped porous carbon and Ru/Cu-doped RuO2 nanocatalysts, significantly improved their ammonia reaction performance by the introduction of ZrO2, and further enhanced the oxygen precipitation performance by Cu doping [28–30]. Alternatively, zinc (Zn)-based materials can promote the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) for the application of catalysis and antibiosis fields [31]. The composites of Ru and Zn metals could not only generate electrochemical oxidization signals, but also may show synergistic catalytic performance for the establishment of dual-mode sensor for 6-MP detection.

Herein, RuZn NPs were firstly prepared and showed burr-like morphology. RuZn NPs exhibited intense electrochemical oxidization signal at 0.78 V with a peak current intensity of 39.0 μA, due to the electron transfer process from Ru0 to Rux+. Importantly, RuZn NPs demosntrated well oxidase-like properties, and were able to catalytic the formation of 1O2 from dissolved oxygen (DO) in water and further promoted the oxidation of TMB to form blue ox-TMB. The unique burr-like configuration of RuZn nanostructures allowed the composites to expose more Ru0 sites for the obtain of high electrochemical oxidation signal, and also enhanced the oxidase-like ability for the synergistic catalytic oxidization of TMB. The affinity between the sulfhydryl groups of 6-MP and RuZn nanostructures induced the decrease of electrochemical oxidation signals and the inactivation of oxidase-like properties, and RuZn NPs engineered electrochemical/colorimetric sensor achived the dual-mode monitoring of 6-MP.

Ruthenium acetylacetonate (Ru(acac)3), zinc acetylacetonate (Zn(acac)2), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys), ascorbic acid (AA), histidine (His), sucrose (SUC), threonine (Thr), tryptophan (Trp), adenine (A), cytosine (C), thymine (T) and guanine (G) were all purchased from Shanghai Titan Technology Co. Polyvinylpyrrolidone K30 (PVP), benzyl alcohol, acetone, ethanol, o-phenylenediamine (OPD), o-phenylene triol (PMK), tert-butanol (TBA), histidine (His), p-benzoquinone (PBQ), Na2SO4, MgSO4, CaCl2, CrCl3, NaCl, ZnCl2 and KCl were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (Beijing, China). 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) was obtained from Shanghai Energy Chemicals Co. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ) was prepared using the Milli-Q water purification system.

The morphology of nanomaterials was characterized by JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM) at 200 kV. The elemental mapping of nanomaterials was tested using JEOL JEMARM200CF STEM (JEOL, Japan). The UV–vis absorption spectra of nanomaterials were recorded with a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2700). The electrochemical properties of the nanomaterials were recorded by CHI-650D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai, China CH Instruments Co.). The glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was obtained from China Tianjin Aida Hengxin Technology Co.

160 mg of PVP was dissolved in 10 mL of benzyl alcohol solution, and then 10 mg of Ru(acac)3 and 6.6 mg of Zn(acac)3 were added. The mixed solution was sonicated for 30 min and heated at 170 °C for 5 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, RuZn NPs were centrifuged with ethyl acetate at 10,000 rpm (v/v = 8/1) and dispersed in 3 mL of ethanol. Ru NPs and Zn NPs were synthesized in the same way by using 10 mg Ru(acac)3 and 6.6 mg Zn(acac)2 as precursors, respectively.

The electrochemical signals of RuZn NPs were tested in an electrochemical workstation. GCE (0.4 mm diameter) was working electrode, and Ag/AgCl electrode using a saturated KCl solution was reference electrode, and platinum wire was counter electrode. The working electrode was polished and cleaned with ultrapure water and ethanol. 5 μL of RuZn NPs were mixed with 5 μL of 0.5 wt% Nafion ethanol solution, and all of them were modified on the GCE surface. The buffer used in the electrochemical tests was 0.1 M pH 7.4 phosphate buffer solution (PBS), and the electrochemical responses of the RuZn NPs were tested using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) at a scan rate of 0.4 mV S−1. The initial potential was set at 0.2 V and the termination potential was set at 1.4 V. The electrochemical signals of Ru NPs and Zn NPs were also tested in the same way.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The oxidase-like performances of RuZn NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic system was constructed in 1 mL 0.1 M pH 7.4 PBS solution by adding 30 μL phosphoric acid. 10 mM TMB was dissolved in DMPO. 30 μL of TMB solution and 10 μL of as-prepared RuZn NPs were added to the catalytic system. After 30 min, the UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded. The oxidase-like activity of RuZn NPs was also inverstigated for the catalytic oxidation of OPD and PMK with the concentration of 100 mM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability assessment of the electrochemical activity and oxidase properties of RuZn NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of RuZn NPs was measured by modifying the same amount of RuZn NPs on the surface of eight different GCE. The anti-interference of RuZn NPs was investigated by mixing 45 μL of as-prepared RuZn NPs with 5 μL of 100 μM interfering ions (Mg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Cr3+, K+, Na+, Zn2+, Cl−, SO4 2−), respectively. After 3 h, 5 μL of the solution was mixed with 5 μL of Nafion solution and then all modified on the GCE surface. The differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) signals were recorded. 10 μL of the mixture of RuZn NPs and interfering ions was added to the above TMB catalytic system, and the UV–vis absorption spectra were tested after incubation for 30 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RuZn NPs engineered electrochemical sensor for 6-MP monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: 45 μL of as-prepared RuZn NPs were mixed with 5 μL of different concentrations of 6-MP solution, inculding 20 nM, 50 nM, 100 nM, 500 nM, 1 μM, 20 μM, 50 μM, 100 μM, 200 μM and 500 μM. After 3 h, 5 μL of the above solution was mixed with 5 μL of Nafion solution, and then was modified on the surface of GCE. The DPV signals were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RuZn NPs engineered colorimetric sensor for 6-MP monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: 45 μL of as-prepared RuZn NPs were mixed with 5 μL of different concentrations of 6-MP solution, inculding 1 μM, 10 μM, 20 μM, 50 μM, 100 μM, 200 μM, 500 μM, 1 mM, 5 mM, 10 mM. After 3 h, 10 μL of the above mixtures were added to the catalytic system. After 30 min, a changed solution from colourless to blue could be observed. The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded.

45 μL of as-prepared RuZn NPs were combined with 5 μL of 1 mM coexisting substances in human serum, including GSH, Cys, AA, His, Thr, SUC, Trp, A, C, T and G. In particular, NEM with the final concentration of 5 mM was mixed with GSH and Cys, in order to shield the effect of the sulfhydryl interfering substances. The electrochemical responses and the catalytic oxidization of TMB were measured according to the above procedures.

The serum samples obtained from Wuxi People's Hospital were diluted 50 times with ethanol and then centrifuged at 4000 rpm, and the supernatants were taken as the actual test sample. Sample addition experiments were performed by adding 10 μM, 50 μM and 100 μM 6-MP into serum samples. 45 μL of RuZn NPs was added to the serum sample. After 3 h, the DPV signals and the UV–vis absorption spectra for the catalytic oxidization of TMB were recorded. The Chinese Pharmacopoeia method was also performed for the detection of 6-MP. An amount of 6-MP was dissolved 1 mL 0.1 M HCl and quantitatively diluted with ethanol. A series of different concentrations of 6-MP solution (10 μM, 20 μM, 50 μM, 100 μM and 500 μM) were prepared and the UV–vis absorption peak at 325 nm were recorded. A linear relationship between the logarithmic concentration of 6-MP and the absorbance at 325 nm was established. The spiked concentrations of 6-MP were determined and compared by the Chinese Pharmacopoeia method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of RuZn NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Ru NPs, Zn NPs and RuZn NPs were synthesized by using Ru(acac)3/Zn(acac)2 as precursors, PVP as stabilizers and benzyl alcohol as reducing agents. PVP was added to prevent the agglomeration of NPs. The use of benzyl alcohol allowed the reduction reaction at high temperatures [25]. Ru(acac)3 was reduced to small sized Ru NPs. As shown in Fig. 1 a, Ru NPs had the sizes of around 3.6 nm and the diluted Ru NPs solution appeared dark brown. Zn NPs showed larger size of around 232.9 nm and better dispersion than Ru NPs (Fig. 1b). When Ru(acac)3 and Zn(acac)2 were both added to the reaction, RuZn NPs were formed and displayed a light brown color in solution. Interestingly, RuZn NPs demonstrated flower structures and possessed well dispersion with the sizes of approximately 87.3 nm (Fig. 1c). As demonstrated in the elemental mapping analysis of Fig. 1d–g, Ru elements were located around the Zn cores. The content of Ru elements in RuZn NPs was less than Zn elements by energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS) characterization (Fig. 1h). The mass ratio of Ru and Zn elements was about 36.7 %, and the molar ratios were 19.2 % and 80.8 % for Ru and Zn elements, respectively. This indicated that both Ru and Zn elements existed in the structures of RuZn NPs, and the fabrication of RuZn NPs could largely reduce the usage of novel metals of Ru. The UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. S1 showed that RuZn NPs had a high absorbance ranging from 250 nm to 500 nm compared to Ru NPs and Zn NPs.

The FT-IR spectra of Zn NPs, Ru NPs and RuZn NPs were provided in Fig. S3a The FT-IR spectra of Ru NPs, Zn NPs and RuZn NPs were similar in the range of 500 cm−1 and 4000 cm−1. The strong infrared band at 3423 cm−1 corresponded to the -N-H stretching vibration of amines, while the band at 2918 cm−1 corresponded to carbon-hydrogen bond stretching. The bands at 1652 and 1384 cm−1 were due to the –C=C stretching and NO2 stretching, respectively. A new peak at 1660 cm−1 for PVP appeared [32,33]. From this, we could judge that PVP had successfully combined on the surface of Zn NPs, Ru NPs and RuZn NPs. The X-ray diffraction patterns of Ru NPs, Zn NPs and RuZn NPs were also characterized in Fig. S3b. For Ru NPs and RuZn NP, a weak peak at 38.10° corresponded to the (100) crystal face of Ru, and peaks near 42.20° and 44.10° corresponded to the (002) and (101) crystal face of Ru (Ru PDF# 06-0663). For Zn NPs and RuZn NP, the peaks at around 31.70°, 34.60°, 36.30°, 47.80°, 56.70°, 67.90° and 69.70° corresponded to the crystal surfaces of [100], [002], [101], [102], [110], [112], [201] (ZnO PDF# 36-1451).

The valence states of Ru and Zn elements in the structures of RuZn NPs were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As seen in Fig. 2 a, the characteristic peak at 285.6 eV was attributed to 3d3/2 orbital peaks of Ru0 [34]. While the peak at 287.4 eV was attributed to the 3d3/2 orbital peaks of Ru4+. By fitting the Ru 3p orbital, the characteristic peak at 460.8 eV and 463.3 eV belonged to the Ru0 3p3/2 orbital and Ru4+ 3p3/2 orbital (Fig. 2b) [35–37]. Fig. 2c showed the XPS pattern of Zn 2p. Comparison with the XPS standard pattern, the characteristic peaks with binding energies of 1021.4 eV and 1044.4 eV were due to the Zn2+ 2p3/2 and Zn2+ 2p1/2 orbitals, respectively. It could be seen that both Ru and Zn elements existed in the structures of RuZn NPs, and were mostly present in the low valence states, allowing for the exploration of possible electrochemical oxidation properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical oxidation properties of RuZn NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical oxidation signals of Ru NPs, Zn NPs and RuZn NPs were tested, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2d, the electrochemical oxidation signals appeared at 0.78 V for both Ru NPs and RuZn NPs, attributing to the electrochemical oxidation process of Ru0 [24]. Whereas, Zn NPs did not have any significant electrochemical oxidation signal. In contrast, the electrochemical reduction signals of Ru NPs, Zn NPs and RuZn NPs were also investigated. As demonstrated in Fig. 2e, there were no any reduction peaks for Ru NPs and Zn NPs, and a very weak reduction peak appeared at 0.77 V for RuZn NPs, due to the presence of a small amount of Ru4+. But the reduction peak was very low, which was approximately one tenth of the oxidation peak of RuZn NPs (Fig. S2). Alternatively, the strong electrochemical oxidation peak enabled RuZn NPs to be the promising electroactive nanotags for the detection applications. Importantly, in comparison to Ru NPs, RuZn NPs had a stronger electrochemical oxidation signal with the peak intensity of 39.0 μA, which was about 1.74 times higher than that of Ru NPs (22.4 μA) (Fig. 2d, Fig. S2). Interestingly, even though RuZn NPs possessed less contents of Ru elements, strong electrochemical oxidation peak intensity still ocurred. From the TEM image in Fig. 1c and the elements mapping in Fig. 1d–g, we can see that Ru wraped around the Zn cores in a burr-like manner to form a core-shell nanostructure. This unique burr-like structures allowed more Ru0 sites to expose on the outside of NPs, which enabled RuZn NPs to generate strong electrochemical oxidation signal. Therefore, the design of RuZn NPs not only reduced the usage of Ru, but also demonstrated high electrochemical oxidation signals for the detection applications. We also tested the electrochemical oxidation signals of RuZn NPs on eight different GCE, and it could be seen that RuZn NPs showed similar peak intensity (Fig. 2f). RuZn NPs showed stable electrochemical signals within 30 days (Fig. S4a). These results indicated that as-prepared electroactive RuZn NPs had good repeatability and stability, and could be served as stable electroactive tags for the detection applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The oxidase-like properties of RuZn NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Ru-based nanomaterials showed potential catalytic performances. Zn-based nanostructures also generated ROS for the application of catalysis and antibiosis fields. Therefore, RuZn NPs may also display synergistic catalytic colorimetric performance. As shown in Fig. 3 a, only RuZn NPs or TMB alone did not showed any absorption in 0.1 M pH 7.4 PBS solution, whereas, high absorption occurred in the UV–vis spectra when RuZn NPs and TMB both existed in 0.1 M pH 7.4 PBS solution. The strong absorption at 650 nm was attributed to the catalytic oxidization of TMB to ox-TMB, and the color of solution changed from transparent to blue [38,39]. Stability tests were also done on the catalytic performance of RuZn NPs at different days. As demonstrated in Fig. S4b, the sum of absorbance at 370 nm, 453 nm and 650 nm did not change significantly with 30 days. This indicated that RuZn NPs possessed good stability for the sensing applications. Meanwhile, RuZn NPs showed similar catalytic performances with natural light on and off (Fig. S5). This could exclude the photoinduced catalytic mechanism [40]. This indicated that RuZn NPs may possess well oxidase-like properties.

In order to check whether the DO in the water played a critical role for catalytic oxidation of TMB, the catalytic reaction from TMB to ox-TMB was compared with and without N2. As showed in Fig. 3b, the absorption peak was largely reduced when N2 was bubbled to the TMB catalytic system, and the solution became transparent. This was because that DO participated in the catalytic oxidation of TMB to ox-TMB. In order to determine the acting ROS types in the catalytic system, three different trapping agents (TBA for ·OH, His for 1O2 and PBQ for ·O2−), were chosen to test the types of ROS [41–43]. As shown in Fig. 3c, when TBA and PBQ were added to the catalytic system, the UV–vis absorption spectra were not significantly different from the blank samples, and the color of the solutions did not change significantly either. When His was added, the UV–vis absorption peaks decreased and the color of the solution became lighter. 1O2 participated in the catalytic oxidation of TMB to ox-TMB. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was also performed for the distinguish of the types of ROS. It was known that TEMP could trap 1O2. As shown in Fig. 3d, before the addition of RuZn NPs, the detection spectrum showed a straight line. However, when RuZn NPs were added, a signal peak with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1 was detected, which was the ESR spectrum attributed to TEMP-O [44]. This result was consistent with the detection of the UV–vis absorption spectrum in Fig. 3c. Therefore, the DO in water was catalyzed to 1O2 under the catalytic effect of RuZn NPs, and 1O2 further oxidized the TMB to produce blue ox-TMB (Fig. 3e) [45].

The oxidase-like performances of Ru NPs, Zn NPs and RuZn NPs were further compared. As demonstrated in Fig. 3f, strong absorption was observed in the presence of RuZn NPs. The peak intensity at 650 nm in the presence of RuZn NPs was around 5 times higher than that in the presence of Ru NPs. In contrast, there was almost no any absorption in the presence of Zn NPs. As displayed in the insert picture of Fig. 3f, the solution was clear and transparent by using Zn NPs as catalyzers, and light blue was observed for the solution in the presence of Ru NPs. However, the color became darker when RuZn NPs were added. This indicated that RuZn NPs possessed best oxidase-like properties than Ru NPs and Zn NPs. The catclytic capability of Ru NPs, Zn NPs and RuZn NPs were further compared by using two other substrates, such as OPD and PMK. As shown in Figs. S6a–b, red and brown solutions were observed for the catalytic system of OPD and PMK in the presence of RuZn NPs. RuZn NPs possessed better oxidase ability than Ru NPs and Zn NPs, owing to the formation of burr-like structures of RuZn NPs and synergistic catalytic effect between the bimetals of Ru and Zn. Alternatively, the excellent oxidase-like properties and the less usage of Ru as well as no requiration of other oxidants enabled RuZn NPs to be alternative catalyzers for the colorimetric analysis applications.

The catatlytic performances of RuZn NPs were further optimized at different conditions. As shown in Fig. S7a, high absorption peaks at 453 nm and 650 nm were observed after the addition of 50 μL phosphoric acid solution. If the amount of phosphoric acid increased from 60 μL to 90 μL, the absorption peaks at 453 nm and 650 nm decreased, but a new peak at 378 nm appeared and became strong. This was due to the further oxidization of ox-TMB from blue to yellow at low pH condition [45]. As shown in Fig. S7b, the highest summation of absorbance was obtained at pH 6 with the addition of 60 μL phosphoric acid. The reaction time of the catalytic system was then optimized accordingly. As shown in Figs. S8a–b, the color of the reaction system changed from light blue at 10 min to dark blue at 30 min. No obvious changes were observed between 30 min and 40 min. Therefore, 30 min was selected as the final reaction time for the catalytic system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the stability of RuZn NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of electrochemical oxidation and catalytic performances of RuZn NPs were investigated in the presence of 10 μM ions of interfering substances, such as Mg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Cr3+, K+, Na+, Zn2+, Cl−, SO4 2−. These interfering substance ions did not have any electrochemical oxidation signals in the range of 0.2 V–1.4 V (Fig. S9a), and could not catalytic oxidization of TMB (Fig. S9b). As shown in Fig. 4 a and b, the added interfering substances had little interference with the electrochemical oxidation signals of RuZn NPs and had negligible effect on the catalytic oxidization of TMB system. Therefore, it could be learnt that RuZn NPs had well stability and could be applied as dual-mode response probes for the detection applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of dual-mode sensor for 6-MP monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: The strong electrochemical oxidization performances and excellent oxidase-like properties endowed RuZn NPs with dual-mode response signals for 6-MP detection. As illustrated in Fig. 5 , 6-MP tended to react with the active Ru0 sites on the surface of RuZn NPs. This would further inhibit the electrochemical oxidation process from Ru0 to Rux+, and hindered the catalytic oxidization process from low oxidizing DO to highly oxidizing 1O2, resulting in the decrease of DPV signal and oxidase-like activity.

As shown in Fig. 6 a and b, the electrochemical oxidation signal of RuZn NPs at 0.78 V decreased in the presence of 100 μM 6-MP, and the catalytic capacity was also reduced to some extent, owing to the formation of Ru-SH covalent bonding between Ru active sites and sulfhydryl groups of 6-MP [24,46]. As shown in Fig. 6c and d, the DPV signal at 0.78 V gradually weakened with the increasing concentrations of 6-MP from 20 nM to 500 μM, and the UV–vis absorption peak intensity for ox-TMB decreased with the increasing amount of 6-MP from 1 μM to 10 mM. The color of TMB catalytic system changed from blue to light blue. As shown in Fig. 6e and f, an electrochemial signal dependent standard curve was established between the DPV signal at 0.78 V and the logarithm of the 6-MP concentration (lg C6-MP) ranging from 100 nM to 200 μM (R2 = 0.9921). A UV–vis absorption signal dependent standard curve was established between the logarithm of the 6-MP concentration (lg C6-MP) and the summing absorbance (at 370 nm, 453 nm and 650 nm). The sum of the three absorbances had highest value, which significantly improved the sensitivity and the stability of the dual-mode sensor. Detection were achiveved in the range of 10 μM to 5 mM (R2 = 0.9911). The LODs were calculated to be 37 nM (SNR = 3) and 300 nM for electrochemical signal and UV–vis absorption signal, respectively. The electrochemical method was more sensitive to 6-MP than the UV–vis absorption signal, but the UV–vis absorption spectroscopy method was more simple and faster than the electrochemical method, which could be a complemented approach for the electrochemical method for the detection of 6-MP. This dual-mode detection principle would make the detection results more stable and reliable.

The detection selectivity was investigated in the presence of 1 mM co-existing substances, including GSH, Cys, AA, His, Thr, SUC, Trp, A, C, T, and G in human serum. As displayed in Fig. S10, these co-existing substances themselves did not show any intrinsic electrochemical oxidation signals (Fig. S10a, rose pink blocks), and also did not have any oxidase activity for the catalytic oxidization of TMB (Fig. S10b, blue columns). As demonstrated in Fig. S10, when AA, His, Thr, SUC, Trp, A, C, T, and G were present, RuZn NPs still showed high DPV signals at 0.78 V (Fig. S10a, green stars), and possessed well oxidase activity for the catalytic oxidization of TMB (Fig. S10a, red columns). This was because that the detection principle was dependent on the Ru-SH covalent bonding between Ru active sites and sulfhydryl groups of 6-MP. AA, His, Thr, SUC, Trp, A, C, T, and G did not showed sulfhydryl groups and would not affect the detection selectivity. NEM was added to eliminate the interferences from GSH and Cys but not affect the detection selectivity for 6-MP. It was because that NEM had higher affinity towards GSH and Cys than 6-MP, owing to the small steric hindrance between NEM and the sulfhydryls on the alkyl group of GSH and Cys. Whereas, the sulfhydryl group on the benzene ring of 6-MP had a relatively large steric hindrance to binding with NEM [47]. As seen in Figs. S10a–b, the electrochemical oxidation signals as well as the oxidase activity of RuZn NPs showed good stability in the presence of different co-existing substances. RuZn NPs had good selectivity for the detection of 6-MP.

In order to assess the feasibility and practicality, sample addition experiments were performed in serum after the addition of 10, 50 and 100 μM 6-MP. Recoveries for different concentrations of 6-MP by electrochemical and colourimetric methods ranged from 98.4 % ± 2.1 %–101.3 % ± 1.6 % and from 97.2 % ± 2.3 % to 101.5 % ± 1.2 % (Table S1, Figs. S11a–b). The detection results for 6-MP were further confirmed by the Chinese pharmacopoeia method. As demonstrated in Fig. S12a-b, a linear relationship between the logarithmic concentration of 6-MP and the absorbance at 325 nm was established. The detection results were 9.9 μM, 50.7 μM and 99.4 μM, which were similar to the results of this developed dual-mode sensor. These results indicated that RuZn NPs engineered dual-mode sensor had good accuracy and could be used for the 6-MP detection applications in real samples. In comparison to the previous reported methods, this developed bi-modal sensors showed wide detection ranges and low LODs (Table S2), and also provided more detection approaches for the monitoring 6-MP at different requirement occasions.

A dual-mode sensor was proposed for 6-MP monitoring by utilizing RuZn NPs as detection nanotags. The burr-like structures of RuZn NPs induced the expose of more Ru0 sites for the generation of high DPV signal, and the strong oxidase-like performances promoted the catalytic oxidization of TMB. The affinity between the sulfhydryl groups of 6-MP and Ru active sites resulted in the decrease of DPV signal at 0.78 V and the catalytic oxidization ability. RuZn NPs were utilized as electroactive and colorimetric dual-functional nanotags for the dual-mode monitoring of 6-MP. The LODs were 37 nM in the range of 100 nM–200 μM for the electrochemical signal and 300 nM in the range of 10 μM to 5 mM for the UV–vis absorption signals. Dual-mode sensing provided a complementary measurement, and also supplied different monitoring approaches at the different requirement occasions, such as spot rapid diagnosis and even laboratory-level precision testing.

Zhao Jin: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Fang Peng: Resources, Investigation, Data curation. Qiaodan Du: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Dan Liang: Resources. Yuan Zhao: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:Yuan Zhao reports financial support was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledged the financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22278184).
